transcript: [doctor] okay , so our next patient is christopher watson , date of birth 4/12/1934 . mr . watson is a 86-year-old male who returns in follow-up for adult hydrocephalus . this is his first visit since undergoing shunt shunt surgery , excuse me , in august 1 , 2020 . i have followed mr . watson since may 2020 when he first presented with 8 to 10 years of progressive gait impairment , cognitive impairment , and decreased bladder control , we established a diagnosis of adult hydrocephalus with a spinal catheter protocol in june 2020 and he underwent shunt shunt surgery on august 1 , 2020 . medtronic strata programmical shunt in the ventriculoperitoneal configuration was programmed at a level of 2.0 was placed . good morning mr . watson , who is the nice lady you have with you today ? [patient] hi document this is my daughter theresa , she is the one who helps me keep things straight . [doctor] mr . watson , have you been sick or been hospitalized since your shunt shunt surgery 3 months ago ? [patient] not that i recall . no , he has n't . he has been doing much better . [doctor] good . i'm glad to hear that . before surgery , you were having some progressive problems that affected your walking , thinking , and bladder control . are these symptoms better , worse , or unchanged ? his walking has improved . he is now able to walk to the dining room just fine but he could not do that before surgery . he still has times where i have to help him . [patient] that's impressive mr . watson ? [doctor] yeah , i guess . now his bladder control did improve some after surgery but now seems to have leveled out , so he is wearing diapers . [patient] no issues with sores from wearing the diaper and any decreased or painful urination ? [doctor] no , not at all . and how about cognition ? [patient] i think i'm better . i do n't feel lost when talking to someone anymore . [doctor] dan and i both think my sister , hannah summarized it best . she said i feel like i can have a normal conversation with him again . not so wonderful . now , have you had any headaches or pain where the shunt is ? [patient] no , has n't bothered me 1 bit . [doctor] okay and any pain in your belly at all ? [patient] ca n't say that i have . [doctor] okay . sounds like you have been improving . alrighty , mr . watson , i'm gon na step out of the room while my resident comes in to do a few tests for me . is that alright ? [patient] sounds good . [doctor] okay . i'm going to go out and review the ct scan you had last month and then we will chat about it when i come in . alrighty mr . watson , so you definitely have made some improvements since the shunt surgery . now on ct scan though , i do believe i see a tiny bit of blood fluid collection in the right parietal region . [patient] now i do n't believe that that's causing any symptoms and i , it actually was n't documented by the radiologist when he read the ct scan . [doctor] what does that mean ? just that i would like to leave the shunt set for another 2 to 3 months before we go in changing it . like i said , if you're not having any headaches or pain and you have improved , so that's all good for me . so really there is , there is nothing to worry about . alright , so i'll come back in 3 months . [patient] yep , that's right . [doctor] i do have a question for you though . [patient] yeah , of course . [doctor] do you think i will be able to drive again ? man , mr . watson , i knew you were gon na ask me that one . i think at this point , it's unlikely that your movement , speed will improve to the level that would be needed for you to pass the driver's safety evaluation . now , i will say that i do occasionally have patients who surprise me . by improving over a 9 to 12-month range and that , that definitely could be you , you could definitely pass it at that point . so if you improve and we can continue to talk about that , then you know i think it could be possible , and i would be happy to recommend it . now , you could definitely also enroll in a driver's safety program without my recommendation , so you could kind of do that preemptively and then we could continue to watch for your improvement as we go . [patient] okay . i kinda figured that anyway but i just thought i'd ask i do n't like to burn anyone when i just need to run to the store , but i also know that i need to be safe . [doctor] yeah , your safety is our # 1 priority and you know i'm sure your daughters do n't mind as much , right ? they wan na keep you here and have you safe , but i overall am very pleased with how much you have improved . [patient] and so i'm sure when we talked again in 3 months , we'll continue to be on that upper trajectory of improvement . [doctor] i sure hope so don . [patient] we really appreciate all that you've done . do we make the appointment upfront ? [doctor] yeah , that would be great . the girls upfront will take care of you and get you settled for 3 months from now and you both take care , alright ? [patient] thanks . [doctor] thank you . hmmm alrighty so ros is constitutional significant for impaired ability to carry out daily functions negative for fever or unintentional weight loss . gu is significant for urinary incontinence and wearing a diaper . negative for genital sores , decreased or painful output . neurological significant for continued walking impairment , and cognitive impairment . negative for headaches , recent falls , or hallucinations . psychiatric is negative for depression , excessive worrying , or mood swings . let's go ahead and use the short pe , vitals let's see blood pressure is 124/80 , heart rate 64 , respirations 18 , weight 174 pounds and pain is 0 out of 10 . mr . watson is a pleasant and cooperative man who is able to converse easily through though his daughter does add some details . the shunt site was clean , dry , and intact with a confirmed setting of two . [patient] he was tested for recent remote memory , attention span and concentration and fund of knowledge . he scored a 26 out of 30 on the mmse when tested with spelling and 25 out of 30 when tested with calculations . [doctor] of note , he was able to get two of the three memory words with cueing and the third with multiple choice . this was a slight improvement over his initial score of 23 out of 30 with calculations and 24 out of 30 with spelling . and at the time he was unable in the previous testing , excuse me , he was unable to remember any memory words and cueing without and only one with multiple choice . gait tested using the tinetti assessment tool . he was tested without an assistive device and received a gait score of 6-8 out of 12 and a balance score of 12 out of 16 for a total score of 18-20 out of 28 . this is slightly improved from his initial score of 15-17 out of 28 . cranial nerves , pupils are equal . eoms are intact . face symmetric . no dysarthria . motor normal for bulk and strength , coordination slow from no finger-to-nose . of note , the ct ct scan from the head of 10/15/2020 shows a frontal hornstan at the level of . firm and of menroma of 4.6 cm with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 mm . by my reading , there is a tiny amount of blood in the right frontal region with a tiny subdermal collection . this was not noticed or noted by the radiologist who stated in any extra axial fluid collections . there is also substantial small vessel ischemic change . diagnosis adult hydrocephalus , gait impairment , urinary incontinence , and urgency , and cognitive impairment .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Follow up adult hydrocephalus, 3 months status post shunt surgery.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Christopher Watson is an 86-year-old male who returns in follow up of adult hydrocephalus. This is his first visit since undergoing shunt surgery 08/01/2020. He is accompanied by his daughter, Theresa, together they give his history.

I have followed Mr. Watson since May 2020. He presented with 8-10 years of progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control. We established a diagnosis of adult hydrocephalus with the spinal catheter protocol in June 2020 and he underwent shunt surgery on 08/01/2020. A Medtronic Strata programmable shunt in the ventriculoperitoneal configuration programmed at level 2.0 was placed.

Mr. Watson has had no hospitalizations or other illnesses since I last saw him. With respect to his walking, his daughter states that he is now able to walk to the dining room just fine but could not before his surgery. His balance has improved though he still has some walking impairment. With respect to his bladder control, initially there was some improvement, but he has leveled off and he wears a diaper. With respect to his cognition, both Theresa and the patient say that his thinking has improved. The other daughter, Hannah summarized it best according to the two of them, she said "I feel like I can have a normal conversation with him again." Mr. Watson has had no headaches and no pain at the shunt site or in the abdomen.

PAST HISTORY

Urinary incontinence.
Adult hydrocephalus.
Gait impairment.
Cognitive impairment.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

 Constitutional Symptoms: Significant for impaired ability to carry out daily functions. Negative for fever or unintentional weight loss.
 Genitourinary: Significant for urinary incontinence and wearing a diaper. Negative for genital sores, decreased or painful output.
 Neurological: Significant for continued walking impairment and cognitive impairment. Negative for headaches, recent falls, or hallucinations.
 Psychiatric: Negative for depression, excessive worrying, or mood swings.

VITALS

Blood pressure 124/80, heart rate is 64, respiratory rate is 18, weight 174 pounds, and pain is 0/10.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Constitutional
Mr. Watson is a pleasant and cooperative man who is able to converse easily though his daughter adds some details.

Head and Face
The shunt site was clean, dry, and intact and confirmed at a setting of 2.0.

Neurological
Mental status: Tested for recent and remote memory, attention span, concentration, and fund of knowledge. He scored 26/30 on the MMSE when tested with spelling and 25/30 when tested with calculations. Of note, he was able to get two of the three memory words with cuing and the third one with multiple choice. This was a slight improvement over his initial score of 23/30 with calculations and 24/30 with spelling and at that time he was unable to remember any memory words with cuing and only one with multiple choice.
Gait: Tested using the Tinetti assessment tool. He was tested without an assistive device and received a gait score of 6-8/12 and a balance of score of 12/16 for a total score of 18-20/28. This has slightly improved from his initial score of 15-17/28.
Cranial Nerves: Pupils are equal. Extraocular movements are intact. Face symmetric. No dysarthria.
Motor: Normal for bulk and strength.
Coordination: Slow for finger-to-nose.

RESULTS

CT scan of the head from 10/15/2020: It shows a frontal horn span at the level of foramen of Munro of 4.6 cm with a 3rd ventricular contour that is flat with the span of 10 mm. By my reading, there is a tiny amount of blood in the right frontal region with just a tiny subdural collection. This was not noticed by the radiologist who stated no extra axial fluid collections. There is also substantial small vessel ischemic change.

ASSESSMENT

 Adult hydrocephalus.
 Gait impairment.
 Urinary incontinence and urgency.
 Cognitive impairment.

Mr. Watson has made some improvement with his hydrocephalus since shunt surgery. There is a tiny fluid collection in the right parietal region. I do not believe that this tiny amount of fluid is symptomatic, and it was not documented by the radiologist when he read the CT scan.

Mr. Watson asked me about whether he will be able to drive again. Unfortunately, I think it is unlikely that his speed of movement will improve to a level that he will be able to pass a driver's safety evaluation, however, occasionally patients surprise me by improving enough over 9 to 12 months that they are able to pass such a test. I would certainly be happy to recommend such a test if I believe he is likely to pass it and he is always welcome to enroll in a driver's safety program without my recommendation, however, I think it is exceeding unlikely that he has the capability of passing this rigorous test at this time. I also think it is quite likely he will not regain sufficient speed of motion to pass such a test.

PLAN

I had a long discussion with the patient and his daughter. We are all pleased that he has started to make some improvement with his hydrocephalus because of the fluid collection in the right parietal region, I would like to leave the setting at 2.0 for another three months before we consider changing the shunt.

INSTRUCTIONS

Follow up in 3 months.
notes:
Subjective:

HPI: Mr. Watson, an 86-year-old male, returns for follow-up after undergoing shunt surgery on August 1, 2020, for adult hydrocephalus. He initially presented with 8-10 years of progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control. Since surgery, he has experienced improvement in his symptoms, with his walking now improved to the point where he can walk to the dining room without assistance, although he still requires occasional help. His bladder control has also improved, but he now wears diapers. He reports no issues with sores or painful urination. He also reports improved cognitive function, stating he no longer feels lost when conversing with others. His daughter, Theresa, corroborates this improvement.

Past medical history: Adult hydrocephalus with spinal catheter protocol since June 2020, shunt surgery on August 1, 2020.

Review of systems: Negative for headaches, pain, fever, unintentional weight loss, recent falls, hallucinations, depression, excessive worrying, or mood swings. Positive for impaired ability to carry out daily functions, urinary incontinence, and wearing a diaper.

Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]

Objective:

Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/80, heart rate 64, respirations 18, weight 174 pounds, and pain 0 out of 10.

Physical exam: The shunt site was clean, dry, and intact with a confirmed setting of 2.0. Mr. Watson is a pleasant and cooperative man who is able to converse easily, although his daughter adds some details. Cranial nerves, pupils are equal, eoms are intact, face symmetric, no dysarthria, motor normal for bulk and strength, coordination slow from finger-to-nose.

Test Results: The MMSE score was 26 out of 30 on the spelling test and 25 out of 30 on the calculation test, with slight improvement from his initial score. Gait tested using the Tinetti assessment tool resulted in a score of 6-8 out of 12 for gait and 12 out of 16 for balance, for a total score of 18-20 out of 28, which is slightly improved from his initial score. The CT scan from 10/15/2020 shows a frontal hornstan at the level of firm and of menroma of 4.6 cm with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 mm, with a tiny amount of blood in the right frontal region and a tiny subdermal collection.

Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: Mr. Watson is a 86-year-old male with adult hydrocephalus who has experienced significant improvement in his symptoms since shunt surgery, with improved gait and cognitive function, although he still requires occasional assistance and wears diapers for bladder control.

Problem list: Adult hydrocephalus; Gait impairment; Urinary incontinence; Cognitive impairment.

Plan:

I plan to continue monitoring Mr. Watson's condition and schedule a follow-up appointment in 3 months to reassess his progress. I will also recommend that he enroll in a driver's safety program to assess his ability to drive safely.

Follow-up: Schedule a follow-up appointment in 3 months to reassess Mr. Watson's progress and discuss the possibility of him driving again.

Subjective:
HPI: Christopher Watson, an 86-year-old male, presents in follow-up for adult hydrocephalus. He has a history of 8-10 years of progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control. He underwent shunt surgery on August 1, 2020, and has been followed since May 2020. He reports that his walking has improved since surgery, but he still has times where he needs assistance. His bladder control has leveled out, and he is wearing diapers, but he does not experience any discomfort or pain. He reports improved cognition, feeling less lost when talking to someone, and his daughter notes that he is able to have a normal conversation with him again. He denies headaches or pain where the shunt is, as well as pain in his belly. He has not been hospitalized or sick since surgery. Past medical history: Adult hydrocephalus, progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control. Review of systems: Impaired ability to carry out daily functions, urinary incontinence, and wearing a diaper, continued walking impairment, and cognitive impairment. Current medications: NOT MENTIONED.

Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/80, pulse rate 64, respirations 18, weight 174 pounds, and pain 0 out of 10. Physical exam: The shunt site was clean, dry, and intact with a confirmed setting of 2.0. The patient scored 26 out of 30 on the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) with spelling and 25 out of 30 with calculations. He was able to get two of the three memory words with cueing and the third with multiple choice. Gait testing using the Tinetti assessment tool revealed a gait score of 6-8 out of 12 and a balance score of 12 out of 16 for a total score of 18-20 out of 28. Cranial nerves, pupils are equal, and EOMs are intact. Face is symmetric, no dysarthria, and motor is normal for bulk and strength, coordination slow from no finger-to-nose. Test Results: CT scan from 10/15/2020 shows a frontal hornstan at the level of firm and menroma of 4.6 cm with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 mm. There is a tiny amount of blood in the right frontal region with a tiny subdermal collection, and substantial small vessel ischemic change.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has made significant improvements in his gait impairment and cognitive function since shunt surgery, but still experiences urinary incontinence and wearing a diaper.

Problem list: Adult hydrocephalus; Gait impairment; Urinary incontinence; Cognitive impairment.

Plan:
I plan to leave the shunt set for another 2-3 months before re-evaluating the need for further intervention. The patient will be followed up in 3 months.

Follow-up:
The patient will be seen again in 3 months.

Subjective:
HPI: Christopher Watson, an 86-year-old male, presents in follow-up for adult hydrocephalus, 3 months post-shunt surgery. He reports progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control for 8-10 years prior to surgery. Since surgery, his gait has improved, and he is now able to walk to the dining room without assistance, although he still requires help at times. His bladder control has leveled out, and he is wearing diapers. He reports no issues with sores or painful urination. His cognitive function has also improved, and he feels he can have normal conversations without feeling lost. He denies headaches, pain where the shunt is, and abdominal pain.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of adult hydrocephalus, which was diagnosed in June 2020, and underwent shunt surgery on August 1, 2020, with a Medtronic Strata Programmable Shunt in the ventriculoperitoneal configuration, set at 2.0.

Review of systems: The patient reports no fever, unintentional weight loss, headaches, recent falls, or hallucinations.

Current medications: NOT MENTIONED

Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/80, heart rate 64, respirations 18, weight 174 pounds, pain 0 out of 10.

Physical exam: The patient is a pleasant and cooperative man who is able to converse easily, with his daughter adding some details. The shunt site is clean, dry, and intact with a confirmed setting of 2.0. Cranial nerves, pupils are equal, EOMs are intact, face is symmetric, and there is no dysarthria. Motor is normal for bulk and strength, coordination is slow from no finger-to-nose. Gait tested using the Tinetti assessment tool, without an assistive device, received a gait score of 6-8 out of 12 and a balance score of 12 out of 16 for a total score of 18-20 out of 28.

Test Results: CT scan from 10/15/2020 shows a frontal hornstan at the level of firm and of menroma of 4.6 cm with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 mm. There is a tiny amount of blood in the right frontal region with a tiny subdermal collection, not noticed by the radiologist. There is also substantial small vessel ischemic change.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has made significant improvements in gait and cognitive function since shunt surgery, but continues to experience urinary incontinence and urgency.

Problem list: Adult hydrocephalus; Gait impairment; Urinary incontinence; Cognitive impairment

Plan:
I will follow up with the patient in 3 months to re-evaluate his condition and adjust the shunt setting as needed. I will also discuss the possibility of driver's safety evaluation and recommend a driver's safety program.

Follow-up: The patient will be seen in 3 months to re-evaluate his condition and adjust the shunt setting as needed.
